crossref.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
          <jats:sec>
            <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
            <jats:p>Chimeric antigen receptor (CAR)-T cells have been used to treat blood cancers by producing a wide variety of cytokines. However, they are not effective in treating solid cancers and can cause severe side-effects, including cytokine release syndrome. TNF<jats:italic>α</jats:italic> is a tumoricidal cytokine, but it markedly increases the protein levels of cIAP1 and cIAP2, the members of inhibitor of apoptosis protein (IAP) family of E3 ubiquitin ligase that limits caspase-induced apoptosis. Degradation of IAP proteins by an IAP antagonist does not effectively kill cancer cells but enables TNF<jats:italic>α</jats:italic> to strongly induce cancer cell apoptosis. It would be a promising approach to treat cancers by targeted delivery of TNF<jats:italic>α</jats:italic> through an inactive adoptive cell in combination with an IAP antagonist.</jats:p>
          </jats:sec>
          <jats:sec>
            <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
            <jats:p>Human dendritic cells (DCs) were engineered to express a single tumoricidal factor, TNF<jats:italic>α</jats:italic>, and a membrane-anchored Mucin1 antibody scFv, named Mucin 1 directed DCs expressing TNF<jats:italic>α</jats:italic> (M-DCs<jats:sup>TNF</jats:sup>). The efficacy of M-DCs<jats:sup>TNF</jats:sup> in recognizing and treating breast cancer was tested in vitro and in vivo.</jats:p>
          </jats:sec>
          <jats:sec>
            <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
            <jats:p>Mucin1 was highly expressed on the surface of a wide range of human breast cancer cell lines. M-DCs<jats:sup>TNF</jats:sup> directly associated with MDA-MB-231 cells in the bone of NSG mice. M-DCs<jats:sup>TNF</jats:sup> plus an IAP antagonist, SM-164, but neither alone, markedly induce MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cell apoptosis, which was blocked by TNF antibody. Importantly, M-DCs<jats:sup>TNF</jats:sup> combined with SM-164, but not SM-164 alone, inhibited the growth of patient-derived breast cancer in NSG mice.</jats:p>
          </jats:sec>
          <jats:sec>
            <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
            <jats:p>An adoptive cell targeting delivery of TNF<jats:italic>α</jats:italic> combined with an IAP antagonist is a novel effective approach to treat breast cancer and could be expanded to treat other solid cancers. Unlike CAR-T cell, this novel adoptive cell is not activated to produce a wide variety of cytokines, except for additional overexpressed TNF, and thus could avoid the severe side effects such as cytokine release syndrome.</jats:p>
          </jats:sec>
title: Chimeric antigen receptor dendritic cells targeted delivery of a single tumoricidal factor for cancer immunotherapy
